svg.title: PPG — animated tour of tutorials 01→06
svg.desc: A 120-second looping SVG that plays through the six tutorials of the PPG proof of concept. It shows the amplified agentic loop end to end: intent enrichment against the ADR store, deterministic plan linting, capability tickets, in-tool guards, skill governance and ADR authoring — culminating in the tutorial 6 climax where a governed skill drives a real Claude Code session through every gate. A breadcrumb of the six acts sits under the title bar and a purple narration panel at the bottom left explains each step as it happens.

g.text: ✓ validated: the governed loop itself — guidance in, plan linted against ADRs, out-of-scope calls refused, drift measured
✓ validated: a real Claude Code session — locked plan, scoped ticket, every Edit checked by the hook, session binding
✓ validated: invariants still reach a black-box agent via generated files — guidance only, no hard enforcement
✓ validated: a skill's structure, description and security tier — plus its companion Rego policy — before publication
✓ validated: a new ADR is live on both halves — surfaced by /enrich and enforced by the plan linter
✓ validated: the whole chain — publication, guidance, hard lock and in-tool guard fire on one governed session
Act 1 · Tutorial 01 — the manual cycle (curl loop)
Act 2 · Tutorial 02 — Claude Code end to end
Act 3 · Tutorial 03 — GitHub Copilot preflight (soft half only)
Act 4 · Tutorial 04 — the skill governance gate
Act 5 · Tutorial 05 — write your first ADR
Act 6 · Tutorial 06 — a governed skill drives a governed session
governance injected inside each step of the loop — planning, execution, observation
or any LLM in a loop
The four gates a governed session crosses
semantic feedback: not "no", but "here is what is missing"
"Add the Seka payment method to checkout"
black-box agent · compensating control at apply time
✗ description: must start with third-person verb
tier derived from tools mentioned in body
validation server restart · ADR store loaded: 5 invariants · Plan linter ready: 5 policies
"still useful if the model is twice as intelligent?"
The team writes the skill and its policy.
The platform owns the gates. The agent executes — and every step passes through them.
Act 1 — the manual cycle: every call goes through the validation server (curl)
Act 2 — the validation server inside a live Claude Code session (MCP + hook)
Act 3 — Copilot preflight: only the soft half applies
Act 4 — the skills registry publication gate
Act 5 — write an ADR twice: prose for the agent, Rego for the linter
Act 6 — climax: one skill, one prompt, all four gates fire in order
The developer starts the validation server. It loads the ADR store (the
architecture decisions) and compiles the OPA policy linters.
curl POST /enrich — the developer sends a raw intent ("Seka payment").
The validation server searches the ADR store for matching invariants…
…and returns ADR-042 + ADR-070 as amplifier context. This is the
"soft half": guidance injected into the plan, not yet enforcement.
A plan missing its test step is submitted. The OPA linter rejects it
and explains what is missing — semantic feedback, not just a "no".
The corrected 3-step plan passes. The plan is locked and a signed
capability ticket (JWT, 15 min TTL) is returned — the "hard half".
The developer tries to patch a file outside the locked plan:
OUT_OF_PLAN_SCOPE. Deterministic refusal — nothing was executed.
GET /debt_report: the validation server also measures how often sessions
drift from their plans — governance observes as well as blocks.
Same loop, now inside Claude Code. The validation server is wired in as an
MCP server plus a PreToolUse hook — the developer just prompts.
Before planning, the agent itself calls the MCP tool
The validation server returns ADR-042 + ADR-070 and the agent reasons over
these invariants while it writes its implementation plan.
The agent locks its plan through MCP. PLAN_LOCKED: the capability
ticket is saved to .ppg-ticket, scoped to exactly 3 files.
The agent edits an in-scope file. The ppg-guard hook validates the
Edit against the ticket and lets it through silently.
The developer asks for an out-of-scope "quick fix". The hook blocks
the Edit before it runs — exit code 2, nothing was modified.
Tickets are bound to one session: replaying an old ticket in a new
GitHub Copilot cannot call the validation server from inside its loop — it is
a black box. So a preflight adapter runs before the session instead.
The adapter calls /enrich once and writes the invariants into
Copilot reads those repo custom-instruction files: the ADRs still
But with no hook and no ticket, there is no hard half here — only
soft guidance. Enforcement must move to apply time (CI checks).
A team publishes a skill. POST /validate_skill lints the SKILL.md
before it may enter the shared registry — gate 1, publication.
The sloppy payload collects five precise violations: naming,
description, version — and the missing companion Rego policy.
Fixed, the skill passes: SKILL_VALID. The rule now exists twice —
prose the agent reads, Rego the platform verifies.
The linter also derives a security tier from the tools a skill uses:
Read → auto-approve, Edit → CI + Rego gate, Bash → human review.
The developer writes a new architecture decision as two files:
Both files are simply copied into the ADR store directory that
After a restart the validation server loads 5 invariants and 5 plan policies:
the new rule is live in every half of the loop at once.
Soft half — an /enrich call about a "status endpoint" now surfaces
ADR-080 as guidance to any agent that asks.
Hard half — a plan for a new endpoint without api/openapi.yaml is
rejected by the very Rego rule the developer just wrote.
With api/openapi.yaml added, the plan locks. One decision, enforced
in both halves — still useful if the model is twice as smart.
Finale — everything composes. The team's skill add-payment-method
passes the publication gate and enters the registry (gate 1).
The developer types a single command: /add-payment-method Stripe.
From here the governed skill drives the whole session.
Gate 2, soft guidance — the skill makes the agent fetch the
platform invariants before it plans anything.
Gate 3, hard lock — the plan (migration first) passes the OPA
linter and the capability ticket lands in .ppg-ticket.
Gate 4, in-tool guard — edits inside the locked scope pass
…but the moment the agent drifts to internal/auth/login.go,
ppg-guard kills the tool call: exit code 2, nothing modified.
The team writes the skill and its policy. The platform owns the
gates. The agent executes — and every step passes through them.
